original_hypothesis.title: rentalWallet Omitted from _deriveRentalOrderHash Enables Order Hash Collision and State Corruption
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function excludes the rentalWallet field from the EIP-712 encoding despite the type string declaring 'address rentalWallet'. This means RentalOrder structs differing only in rentalWallet produce identical hashes. An attacker calling stopRent can substitute a different rentalWallet in the order struct and still pass the hash existence check in Storage.removeRentals. This causes assets to be reclaimed from the wrong Safe, rentalAsset counters for the wrong wallet to be decremented, and the original wallet's rental counters to remain permanently elevated, locking assets behind the guard forever.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect: the abi.encode call in _deriveRentalOrderHash goes from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp, skipping order.rentalWallet. The hash output is identical regardless of rentalWallet value, allowing callers to supply arbitrary wallet addresses to stopRent while matching stored order hashes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode skips order.rentalWallet: encodes order.renter then order.startTimestamp directly, missing the rentalWallet field between them

preconditions: An active rental order exists in Storage.orders
Attacker knows all fields of the active rental order (emitted in RentalOrderStarted event)
A substitute rental safe (protocol-deployed) holds matching ERC721/ERC1155 tokens for the reclaim to succeed, OR for ERC1155 the rentedAssets count at the substitute wallet must not underflow

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: abi.encode(_RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH, order.seaportOrderHash, ..., order.renter, order.startTimestamp, order.endTimestamp) — rentalWallet absent
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Caller of stopRent provides fabricated rentalWallet that matches a different Safe, while the computed hash still equals the stored order hash
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can force reclaim from wrong safe (stealing ERC721/ERC1155 if present), permanently lock original safe's assets (rentedAssets never decremented), and potentially corrupt escrow settlement

economic_questions: Can an attacker reliably create conditions where a substitute safe holds matching rental tokens?
What is the economic impact of permanently locking assets in the original safe?
Can ERC1155 amounts > 1 create scenarios where the substitute safe's rentedAssets don't underflow?
validation_requirements: Construct two rental safes with same NFT types, verify stopRent succeeds with swapped rentalWallet
Verify rentedAssets for original safe remain elevated after exploit
Check if escrow payment settlement sends funds to correct lender/renter despite wallet swap

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of creating a rental + acquiring matching NFTs for substitute safe
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of stolen NFTs from substitute safe + permanent lockup of original safe assets

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Targeted theft, no market manipulation needed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Owner of substitute safe loses NFTs; original rental participants have assets permanently locked
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of rented NFT value + locked payments
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All active rentals with matching ERC1155 tokens across safes

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare hash output with and without rentalWallet modification
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the abi.encode field ordering against EIP-712 type string

validation_result.mechanism: EIP-712 field omission in rental order hash
validation_result.impact_summary: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function omits order.rentalWallet from its abi.encode call despite the EIP-712 type string declaring it. Two RentalOrder structs differing only in rentalWallet produce identical hashes, enabling stopRent callers to substitute a different rental Safe and cause asset reclamation from the wrong wallet, state corruption of rentedAssets counters, and permanent asset locking.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function in Signer.sol encodes fields in this order: _RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH, seaportOrderHash, items hash, hooks hash, orderType, lender, renter, startTimestamp, endTimestamp. However, the EIP-712 type string for RentalOrder declares fields as: seaportOrderHash, items, hooks, orderType, lender, renter, rentalWallet, startTimestamp, endTimestamp. The rentalWallet field is missing between renter and startTimestamp in the abi.encode call. This means changing rentalWallet does not change the computed hash. In stopRent, the order hash is used to verify the order exists in Storage.orders, and the rentalWallet is used to determine which Safe to reclaim assets from and which rentalIds to decrement. An attacker can supply a modified rentalWallet that still produces the same hash, causing assets to be reclaimed from a different Safe and rental counters for the wrong wallet to be modified.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is unambiguous. Line-by-line comparison of the type string (which includes 'address rentalWallet') against the abi.encode call (which skips from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp) confirms the omission. The stopRent function in Stop.sol uses order.rentalWallet in three critical places: (1) computing rentalAssetUpdates via toRentalId(order.rentalWallet), (2) reclaiming items via ISafe(order.rentalWallet).execTransactionFromModule, and (3) passing to ESCRW.settlePayment. All three operations use the attacker-controlled rentalWallet while the hash check passes against the stored order hash.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
return
    keccak256(
        abi.encode(
            _RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH,
            order.seaportOrderHash,
            keccak256(abi.encodePacked(itemHashes)),
            keccak256(abi.encodePacked(hookHashes)),
            order.orderType,
            order.lender,
            order.renter,
            order.startTimestamp,
            order.endTimestamp
        )
    );
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The abi.encode call transitions from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp, omitting order.rentalWallet. The type string declares 'address rentalWallet' between 'address renter' and 'uint256 startTimestamp'. This means orders with different rentalWallet values produce identical hashes.
The type string explicitly declares 'address rentalWallet' as the 7th field, but the encoding in _deriveRentalOrderHash only encodes 8 values (typehash + 7 fields) instead of 9 (typehash + 8 fields).

stopRent takes the full RentalOrder from calldata (including rentalWallet). It uses order.rentalWallet for computing rentalAssetUpdates (toRentalId), reclaiming items, and settling payments. But the hash check in STORE.removeRentals uses _deriveRentalOrderHash which ignores rentalWallet, so a modified rentalWallet still produces a matching hash.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls Stop.stopRent(order) with modified order.rentalWallet = Safe_B (original was Safe_A)
2. _validateRentalCanBeStoped checks orderType, endTimestamp, lender — all same as original, passes
3. rentalAssetUpdates computed using order.items[i].toRentalId(Safe_B) — produces rentalIds for Safe_B
4. _reclaimRentedItems(order) calls ISafe(Safe_B).execTransactionFromModule with delegatecall to Reclaimer
5. Reclaimer.reclaimRentalOrder checks address(this) != original (passes in delegatecall) and address(this) == order.rentalWallet (Safe_B == Safe_B, passes)
6. NFTs transferred from Safe_B to order.lender
8. STORE.removeRentals(_deriveRentalOrderHash(order), updates) — hash matches stored hash because rentalWallet is excluded
9. Storage deletes order hash and decrements rentedAssets for Safe_B's rentalIds instead of Safe_A's
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: stopRent → _deriveRentalOrderHash (Signer.sol:180-194) produces same hash regardless of rentalWallet → STORE.removeRentals (Storage.sol:216-232) validates orders[orderHash] == true (passes because hash is identical) → deletes order and decrements wrong rentalIds. Meanwhile _reclaimRentedItems (Stop.sol:209-225) reclaims from the substitute Safe_B via ISafe(order.rentalWallet).execTransactionFromModule. The Reclaimer (Reclaimer.sol:80-107) validates address(this) == rentalOrder.rentalWallet which passes because both are Safe_B in delegatecall context.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The defect is in the core hash computation logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible when: (1) An active rental exists (order hash H stored in Storage.orders). (2) Attacker knows all order fields (emitted in RentalOrderStarted event). (3) A different protocol-deployed Safe (Safe_B) holds matching ERC721/ERC1155 tokens. For ERC1155, this naturally occurs when the same token ID is rented across multiple Safes. The attacker calls stopRent with the original order fields but substitutes rentalWallet = Safe_B. The hash matches, the reclaim executes from Safe_B, and the original Safe_A's rentedAssets remain elevated permanently, locking those assets behind the Guard.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. (1) Assets can be reclaimed from wrong rental Safes, effectively stealing NFTs from innocent renters. (2) Original rental Safe's rentedAssets counters remain permanently elevated, causing the Guard to permanently block those assets from being transferred — even after the rental should have ended. (3) Payment settlement may distribute funds incorrectly. (4) The storage order hash is deleted, so the original rental cannot be properly stopped later.
recommended_mitigations: Add order.rentalWallet to the abi.encode call in _deriveRentalOrderHash between order.renter and order.startTimestamp: abi.encode(_RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH, order.seaportOrderHash, keccak256(abi.encodePacked(itemHashes)), keccak256(abi.encodePacked(hookHashes)), order.orderType, order.lender, order.renter, order.rentalWallet, order.startTimestamp, order.endTimestamp)
Audit all other EIP-712 hash functions (_deriveOrderMetadataHash also omits orderType and emittedExtraData) for similar field omission bugs
Add invariant tests that verify hash uniqueness across orders differing in each individual field
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full protocol including two rental Safes (Safe_A, Safe_B). 2. Create rental order with ERC1155 token to Safe_A, record emitted order fields. 3. Transfer or rent the same ERC1155 token ID to Safe_B. 4. Call stopRent with the original order's fields but rentalWallet = Safe_B. 5. Verify: (a) hash matches stored hash, (b) ERC1155 transferred from Safe_B, (c) Safe_A's rentedAssets still > 0, (d) Safe_A's assets permanently locked by Guard.
uncertainties: For ERC721, the attack requires the substitute Safe to hold the exact same unique token, which is harder to arrange than for ERC1155
The practical exploitability depends on whether matching tokens exist in another protocol-deployed Safe at the time of the attack
additional_testing_needed: Verify the exact behavior when the substitute Safe doesn't hold the expected tokens (should revert entire tx)
Test with ERC1155 tokens rented across multiple Safes to confirm the attack path end-to-end
Verify that permanently elevated rentedAssets truly prevents all transfers via the Guard
Check if there's any admin mechanism to correct corrupted rentedAssets state
